competency_label: Distributed Coordination — Remote, Hybrid, and Cross-Time-Zone Discipline

dimensions.name: async_with_context
dimensions.description: Does the learner collapse the stalled cross-time-zone decision loop with a single fully-contexted asynchronous message — context + the options + a recommendation + a clear by-when and reply format — assuming the next response is ~24h away, rather than firing a context-light one-line question (which keeps the loop spinning) or defaulting to yet another hard-to-align call?

anchors.1: Defaults to 'let's all get on a call' for the decision, or sends another bare one-line question that won't collapse the 24h loop.
anchors.3: Moves it to async but the message is thin — missing the recommendation, the by-when, or a clear reply format, so the loop may persist.
anchors.5: Sends one async message carrying full context + options + a recommendation + a clear by-when and reply format, designed to resolve in a single round-trip.

dimensions.name: written_memory
dimensions.description: Does the learner write down and circulate the verbally-agreed decision (the IdP-for-phase-1 call) so the distributed team shares one record — replacing the ambient memory a co-located team would hold collectively — rather than leaving a consequential decision living only in someone's recollection of a call?

anchors.1: Leaves the verbal decision unwritten, or adds no written confirmation of consequential agreements.
anchors.3: Writes something down but partially — doesn't circulate it, or doesn't resolve the conflicting understandings.
anchors.5: Writes the decision down clearly and circulates it to both sites to reconcile the differing understandings, creating shared memory.

dimensions.name: meeting_judgment
dimensions.description: Does the learner choose async vs meeting deliberately — reserving real-time meetings for what genuinely needs them, and, where a meeting is warranted, giving it a tight agenda and structure for distributed attention — rather than reflexively scheduling calls?

anchors.1: Reflexively schedules meetings for things that could be async; no structure.
anchors.3: Roughly the right instinct on async-vs-meeting but applied loosely, or a meeting without distributed-attention structure.
anchors.5: Deliberately routes the decision and recap to async; reserves/structures any meeting that genuinely needs real-time, with a tight agenda.

dimensions.name: timezone_fairness
dimensions.description: Does the learner notice and fix the burden that has fallen on one zone (Toronto taking the 2am sync for two months) — rotate it, or otherwise share the inconvenience, and acknowledge accommodations — rather than ignoring it or offloading the pain onto the distant team?

anchors.1: Ignores the rotation problem, or actively keeps/dumps the unsocial hour on one zone ('they'll cope').
anchors.3: Notices the fairness issue but the fix is vague or only half-addresses it.
anchors.5: Rotates the unsocial slot (or proposes a concrete fair pattern) and explicitly acknowledges who's been carrying the accommodation.

dimensions.name: human_layer
dimensions.description: Does the learner make the light-touch human move that a shared office would produce automatically — a short, non-transactional check-in with the teammate who has gone quiet (Arjun) — replacing the ambient awareness remote work loses, without being heavy-handed or making it a performance issue?

anchors.1: Ignores the quiet teammate entirely, or treats it only as a performance/output problem.
anchors.3: Notes the teammate but the response is perfunctory or framed mainly around their deliverables.
anchors.5: Makes a genuine, light-touch personal check-in with the quiet teammate (and/or a discreet flag to the PM), human-first not output-first.
